overview.title: Ransomware Attack on Healthcare Network via Phishing and Malware Deployment
overview.summary: A sophisticated ransomware attack targeting a healthcare organization begins with a phishing campaign, followed by the deployment of custom malware to gain initial access. The attackers demonstrate advanced persistence, privilege escalation, and lateral movement techniques to achieve their goal of data exfiltration and service disruption.

threat_actor_profile.motivation: Financial gain through ransom demands and data exfiltration for sale on darknet markets.

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.description: The attackers gather intelligence on the target organization's infrastructure and employees using open-source intelligence (OSINT) techniques, focusing on publicly available information to identify potential entry points and internal structures.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 673 Name: Developer Signing Maliciously Altered Software
stage.description: The attackers develop custom malware tailored for the attack, designed to bypass standard security measures and remain undetected during initial stages.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.description: Attackers send malicious emails containing links to a fake login page or attachments with embedded scripts to trick healthcare staff into revealing credentials or allowing execution of malicious code.

stage.name: Execution
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 253 Name: Remote Code Inclusion
stage.description: Exploitation of a vulnerability in a web application allows the attackers to execute arbitrary code, establishing a foothold in the network.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 645 Name: Use of Captured Tickets (Pass The Ticket)
stage.description: Attackers create a new account with elevated privileges to maintain persistent access to the network, ensuring long-term presence despite potential reboots or disconnections.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 226 Name: Session Credential Falsification through Manipulation
stage.description: Extracting credentials from memory to escalate privileges within the network, enabling access to higher-privilege systems and data.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.description: Injecting malicious code into legitimate processes to evade detection by antivirus and endpoint protection solutions.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.description: Discovering password policies to exploit weak points in the authentication process, enabling further unauthorized access.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 309 Name: Network Topology Mapping
stage.description: Mapping the network to identify critical assets and services, preparing for lateral movement and data collection.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 555 Name: Remote Services with Stolen Credentials
stage.description: Using RDP to move laterally between systems within the network, expanding the scope of the attack.

stage.name: Collection
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 24 Name: Filter Failure through Buffer Overflow
stage.description: Collecting sensitive patient data and exfiltrating it securely using custom scripts to avoid detection.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 609 Name: Cellular Traffic Intercept
stage.description: Transferring stolen data out of the network using encryption to avoid detection, potentially leveraging cellular networks for exfiltration.

stage.name: Impact
stage.description: Tampering with critical healthcare systems to cause operational disruption, potentially leading to delays in patient care and data loss.

Scenario.summary: The attack begins with reconnaissance and resource development, followed by initial access via phishing. Execution and persistence are established, leading to privilege escalation and defense evasion. Credential access and discovery enable lateral movement across the network, culminating in data collection, exfiltration, and ultimately, significant disruption to healthcare operations.
